Any S3-compatible client — boto3, AWS CLI, DuckDB, rclone — works without modification.\n6\t\n7\t- **Zero-knowledge encryption** — data is encrypted before it leaves ARMOR; B2 only ever stores ciphertext\n8\t- **Zero egress fees** — downloads route through Cloudflare via the Bandwidth Alliance\n9\t- **Seekable encryption** — AES-256-CTR with 64KB blocks enables byte-range reads without decrypting the whole file\n10\t- **DuckDB-compatible** — query encrypted Parquet files with column pruning and predicate pushdown intact\n11\t- **Multi-key routing** — different master keys for different path prefixes; automatic key selection per object\n12\t\n13\t## Quick Start\n14\t\n15\t### Docker\n16\t\n17\t```bash\n18\tdocker run -d \\\n19\t  -p 9000:9000 \\\n20\t  -p 9001:9001 \\\n21\t  -e ARMOR_B2_REGION=us-east-005 \\\n22\t  -e ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your-key-id \\\n23\t  -e ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your-key-secret \\\n24\t  -e ARMOR_BUCKET=your-bucket \\\n25\t  -e ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN=your-cf-domain.example.com \\\n26\t  -e ARMOR_MEK=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \\\n27\t  -e ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY=my-access-key \\\n28\t  -e ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY=my-secret-key \\\n29\t  ronaldraygun/armor:0.1.43\n30\t```\n31\t\n32\t> **Note:** The ARMOR CI pipeline auto-bumps the VERSION file on every build and publishes the container image as `ronaldraygun/armor:<version>` to Docker Hub. Always pin to a specific version tag in production deployments. Use the latest published tag from [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/r/ronaldraygun/armor/tags).\n33\t\n34\t### Client Configuration\n35\t\n36\tPoint any S3-compatible tool at ARMOR's listen address:\n37\t\n38\t```bash\n39\t# AWS CLI\n40\taws --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3 cp file.txt s3://bucket/key\n41\t\n42\t# boto3 (Python)\n43\timport boto3\n44\ts3 = boto3.client('s3',\n45\t    endpoint_url='http://localhost:9000',\n46\t    aws_access_key_id='my-access-key',\n47\t    aws_secret_access_key='my-secret-key')\n48\ts3.upload_file('local.txt', 'bucket', 'key')\n49\t\n50\t# DuckDB\n51\tINSTALL httpfs;\n52\tLOAD httpfs;\n53\tSET s3_endpoint='localhost:9000';\n54\tSET s3_access_key_id='my-access-key';\n55\tSET s3_secret_access_key='my-secret-key';\n56\tSELECT * FROM read_parquet('s3://bucket/data.parquet');\n57\t```\n58\t\n59\t## Cost Model\n60\t\n61\t| Component | Cost |\n62\t|-----------|------|\n63\t| Storage | ~$6–7/TB/month |\n64\t| Egress (via Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance) | $0 |\n65\t| B2 API calls | $0 |\n66\t| Cloudflare (free plan) | $0 |\n67\t| **Total** | **~$6–7/TB/month** |\n68\t\n69\t## Architecture\n70\t\n71\t### Upload Path (direct to B2 — ingress is free)\n72\t\n73\t```\n74\t┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n75\t│  Client   │────▶│    ARMOR     │────▶│    B2    │\n76\t│           │     │  encrypt +   │     │          │\n77\t│           │     │  upload      │     │          │\n78\t└──────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┘\n79\t```\n80\t\n81\t### Download Path (through Cloudflare — egress is free)\n82\t\n83\t```\n84\t┌──────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n85\t│  Client   │◀───│ Cloudflare │◀───│  Cloudflare │◀───│    B2    │\n86\t│  ARMOR    │    │   Edge     │    │  PNI Link   │    │          │\n87\t│  decrypt  │    │  (cache)   │    │  (free)     │    │          │\n88\t└──────────┘     └────────────┘     └────────────┘     └──────────┘\n89\t```\n90\t\n91\t### DuckDB Query Path (seekable decryption)\n92\t\n93\tDuckDB issues byte-range GET requests for specific row groups and columns. ARMOR decrypts only the requested 64KB blocks, so column pruning and predicate pushdown remain effective:\n94\t\n95\t```\n96\tDuckDB                          ARMOR                       Cloudflare → B2\n97\t  │                                │                              │\n98\t  ├─ read footer (last 8 bytes) ──▶├─ Range GET (encrypted) ────▶│\n99\t  │◀── decrypted footer ──────────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n100\t  │                                │                              │\n101\t  ├─ read col_a, row group 3 ────▶├─ Range GET (3 blocks) ─────▶│\n102\t  │◀── decrypted column chunk ────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n103\t  │                                │                              │\n104\t  └─ result set                    └                              └\n105\t```\n106\t\n107\t## Encryption Design\n108\t\n109\t```\n110\tMaster Key (MEK)\n111\t │  stored locally, never uploaded\n112\t │\n113\t └─▶ wraps ──▶ Data Encryption Key (DEK)\n114\t                │  random per-file, wrapped copy in B2 metadata\n115\t                │\n116\t                └─▶ encrypts ──▶ File Data\n117\t                                   AES-256-CTR, 64KB blocks\n118\t                                   per-block HMAC-SHA256\n119\t                                   seekable random access\n120\t```\n121\t\n122\tKey rotation re-wraps DEKs without re-uploading file data — a metadata-only operation.\n123\t\n124\t## Security Model\n125\t\n126\t| Threat | Mitigation |\n127\t|--------|-----------|\n128\t| B2 breach | Client-side encryption — B2 only stores opaque blobs |\n129\t| Cloudflare inspection | Client-side encryption — Cloudflare only caches opaque blobs |\n130\t| Man-in-the-middle | TLS everywhere + client-side encryption |\n131\t| Key compromise | Envelope encryption — per-file DEKs limit blast radius; rotation re-wraps without re-uploading |\n132\t| Data corruption | SHA-256 integrity hash + per-block HMACs |\n133\t| Unauthorized access | Private bucket + Cloudflare Worker auth + scoped application keys |\n134\t\n135\t## Configuration\n136\t\n137\tARMOR is configured via environment variables:\n138\t\n139\t| Variable | Required | Default | Description |\n140\t|----------|----------|---------|-------------|\n141\t| `ARMOR_LISTEN` | No | `0.0.0.0:9000` | S3 API listen address |\n142\t| `ARMOR_ADMIN_LISTEN` | No | `127.0.0.1:9001` | Admin API (key rotation, canary, audit) |\n143\t| `ARMOR_B2_REGION` | Yes | — | B2 region (e.g., `us-east-005`) |\n144\t| `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | Yes | — | B2 application key ID |\n145\t| `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | Yes | — | B2 application key |\n146\t| `ARMOR_BUCKET` | Yes | — | B2 bucket name |\n147\t| `ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN` | Yes | — | Cloudflare domain CNAME'd to B2 |\n148\t| `ARMOR_MEK` | Yes | — | Master encryption key (hex, 32 bytes) |\n149\t| `ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY` | No | (random) | Client access key |\n150\t| `ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY` | No | (random) | Client secret key |\n151\t| `ARMOR_BLOCK_SIZE` | No | `65536` | Encryption block size (bytes) |\n152\t| `ARMOR_WRITER_ID` | No | (hostname) | Provenance chain writer ID |\n153\t| `ARMOR_READYZ_CACHE_TTL` | No | `30` | Seconds to cache backend connectivity in `/readyz` |\n154\t| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_USER` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth username |\n155\t| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_PASS` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth password |\n156\t| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_TOKEN` | No | — | Dashboard Bearer token |\n157\t\n158\t### Multi-Key Routing\n159\t\n160\tRoute different path prefixes to different master keys:\n161\t\n162\t```bash\n163\tARMOR_MEK=<hex>                           # default key\n164\tARMOR_MEK_SENSITIVE=<hex>                 # named key\n165\tARMOR_MEK_ARCHIVE=<hex>                   # named key\n166\tARMOR_KEY_ROUTES=\"data/pii/*=sensitive,archive/*=archive,*=default\"\n167\t```\n168\t\n169\t### Multi-Credential Configuration\n170\t\n171\t```bash\n172\tARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACCESS_KEY=reader-key\n173\tARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_SECRET_KEY=reader-secret\n174\tARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACL=\"mybucket:readonly/*\"\n175\t\n176\tARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACCESS_KEY=writer-key\n177\tARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_SECRET_KEY=writer-secret\n178\tARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACL=\"mybucket:*,otherbucket:uploads/*\"\n179\t```\n180\t\n181\t## S3 API Coverage\n182\t\n183\t### Transforming Operations (encryption/decryption applied)\n184\t\n185\t| Operation | Support |\n186\t|-----------|---------|\n187\t| PutObject | Full (streaming for large files) |\n188\t| GetObject | Full (range reads) |\n189\t| HeadObject | Full (plaintext size, conditionals) |\n190\t| CopyObject | Full (DEK re-wrapping, cross-bucket) |\n191\t| CreateMultipartUpload | Full |\n192\t| UploadPart | Full |\n193\t| CompleteMultipartUpload | Full |\n194\t| AbortMultipartUpload | Full |\n195\t| ListParts | Full |\n196\t| ListMultipartUploads | Full |\n197\t\n198\t### Passthrough Operations\n199\t\n200\t| Operation | Support |\n201\t|-----------|---------|\n202\t| ListObjectsV2 | Full (size correction, `.armor/` filter) |\n203\t| DeleteObject | Full |\n204\t| DeleteObjects | Full |\n205\t| ListBuckets | Full |\n206\t| CreateBucket / DeleteBucket / HeadBucket | Full |\n207\t| Lifecycle configuration | Full |\n208\t| Object Lock / Retention / Legal Hold | Full |\n209\t\n210\t**Reserved Namespace: `.armor/`**\n211\t\n212\tThe `.armor/` prefix is reserved for ARMOR internal use. Client operations targeting keys with this prefix return `403 AccessDenied`. This protects:\n213\t\n214\t- `.armor/chain/<writer>/*` — Tamper-evident provenance chain entries\n215\t- `.armor/chain-head/<writer>` — Provenance chain head pointers\n216\t- `.armor/manifest/<writer>/*` — Manifest delta files (IV + wrapped DEK entries)\n217\t- `.armor/hmac/<sha256>` — Multipart upload HMAC sidecars\n218\t- `.armor/rotation-state.json` — In-progress key rotation state\n219\t- `.armor/multipart/*.state` — Crash recovery state for multipart uploads\n220\t- `.armor/canary/*` — Health check canary objects\n221\t\n222\tInternal ARMOR components (provenance recorder, manifest persistence, canary, key rotation, multipart state manager) access these keys directly through the backend layer, bypassing the S3 handler guard.\n223\t\n224\t## Web Dashboard\n225\t\n226\tA web dashboard for bucket browsing, encryption status, and metrics is available on the admin port (default `127.0.0.1:9001`):\n227\t\n228\t```bash\n229\topen http://localhost:9001/dashboard\n230\t```\n231\t\n232\tFeatures:\n233\t- Bucket browsing with prefix-based navigation\n234\t- Encryption status badges per object (key name, ARMOR vs. unencrypted)\n235\t- Metadata cache hit rates\n236\t- Real-time metrics: requests, bytes transferred, uptime, canary status\n237\t\n238\tSee [docs/dashboard.md](docs/dashboard.md) for full documentation.\n239\t\n240\t## Admin API\n241\t\n242\tKey management and monitoring endpoints on the admin listener (`127.0.0.1:9001`):\n243\t\n244\t| Endpoint | Method | Description |\n245\t|----------|--------|-------------|\n246\t| `/healthz` | GET | Liveness check |\n247\t| `/readyz` | GET | Readiness check (verifies B2 connectivity) |\n248\t| `/metrics` | GET | Prometheus metrics |\n249\t| `/admin/key/verify` | GET | Verify MEK can decrypt the canary object |\n250\t| `/admin/key/rotate` | POST | Rotate MEK — re-wraps all DEKs, no file re-upload |\n251\t| `/admin/key/export` | GET | Export current MEK (`?confirm=yes`) |\n252\t| `/admin/audit` | GET | Walk provenance chains, verify integrity |\n253\t| `/admin/presign` | POST | Generate pre-signed share URL |\n254\t| `/armor/canary` | GET | Canary integrity status |\n255\t| `/dashboard` | GET | Web dashboard |\n256\t\n257\t## Repository Structure\n258\t\n259\t```\n260\tARMOR/\n261\t├── cmd/armor/main.go          # Entrypoint\n262\t├── internal/\n263\t│   ├── server/                # S3 API handlers, auth\n264\t│   ├── crypto/                # Encryption, decryption, envelope key management\n265\t│   ├── backend/               # B2 S3 client, Cloudflare download routing\n266\t│   ├── canary/                # Self-healing integrity monitor\n267\t│   ├── config/                # Configuration loading (env vars)\n268\t│   ├── keymanager/            # Multi-key routing\n269\t│   ├── dashboard/             # Web dashboard UI and metrics\n270\t│   ├── presign/               # Pre-signed URL generation\n271\t│   ├── provenance/            # Cryptographic audit chain\n272\t│   ├── logging/               # Structured JSON logging\n273\t│   └── metrics/               # Prometheus metrics\n274\t├── deploy/kubernetes/         # Kubernetes manifests\n275\t├── tests/integration/         # Integration tests (requires real B2 + Cloudflare)\n276\t└── docs/\n277\t    ├── dashboard.md\n278\t    ├── cloudflare-setup.md    # DNS configuration for zero-egress downloads\n279\t    └── research/\n280\t```\n281\t\n282\t## Documentation\n283\t\n284\t- [Disaster Recovery](docs/disaster-recovery.md) — MEK backup/escrow, restore drills, rotation failure recovery\n285\t- [Web Dashboard](docs/dashboard.md) — Bucket browsing, encryption status, cache statistics\n286\t- [Cloudflare Setup](docs/cloudflare-setup.md) — DNS configuration for zero-egress B2 downloads\n287\t- [Integration Tests](tests/integration/README.md) — Testing against real B2 + Cloudflare\n288\t\n289\t## Disaster Recovery / Offline Decryption\n290\t\n291\tARMOR includes a standalone CLI tool `armor-decrypt` for recovering encrypted objects without a running ARMOR server. This enables disaster recovery scenarios where you have:\n292\t\n293\t- The Master Encryption Key (MEK)\n294\t- Access to B2 (or a local copy of an encrypted object)\n295\t\n296\t### Building the Decrypt Tool\n297\t\n298\t```bash\n299\tgo build -o armor-decrypt ./cmd/armor-decrypt\n300\t```\n301\t\n302\t### Usage\n303\t\n304\t#### Decrypt from B2\n305\t\n306\t```bash\n307\t# Decrypt directly from B2 (requires B2 credentials)\n308\tarmor-decrypt \\\n309\t  -mek 0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef \\\n310\t  -input b2://my-bucket/path/to/file.encrypted \\\n311\t  -output recovered-file.txt\n312\t\n313\t# Using MEK from environment\n314\texport ARMOR_MEK=0123456789abcdef...\n315\tarmor-decrypt -input b2://my-bucket/file -output recovered.txt\n316\t\n317\t# With verbose output\n318\tarmor-decrypt -mek HEX -input b2://bucket/file -v - output recovered.txt\n319\t```\n320\t\n321\t#### Decrypt from Local File\n322\t\n323\tFor local files, you need the wrapped DEK (from `x-amz-meta-armor-wrapped-dek` metadata):\n324\t\n325\t```bash\n326\tarmor-decrypt \\\n327\t  -mek 0123456789abcdef... \\\n328\t  -input /path/to/encrypted.bin \\\n329\t  -wrapped-dek WWF...base64... \\\n330\t  -output plaintext.bin\n331\t```\n332\t\n333\t### Key Requirements\n334\t\n335\t- **MEK (Master Encryption Key)**: 32-byte hex string\n336\t- **For B2**: `ARMOR_B2_REGION`, `ARMOR_B2_ENDPOINT`, `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`\n337\t- **For local files**: Wrapped DEK (base64, from object metadata)\n338\t\n339\t### Multi-Key Support\n340\t\n341\tIf your ARMOR deployment uses named keys (via `ARMOR_KEY_ROUTES`), specify the key ID:\n342\t\n343\t```bash\n344\tarmor-decrypt \\\n345\t  -mek <hex-for-specific-key> \\\n346\t  -input b2://bucket/file \\\n347\t  -key-id sensitive \\\n348\t  -output recovered.txt\n349\t```\n350\t\n351\tThe key ID comes from the `x-amz-meta-armor-key-id` metadata header.\n352\t\n353\t### Verification\n354\t\n355\tThe decrypt tool automatically:\n356\t\n357\t- Verifies per-block HMAC-SHA256 integrity\n358\t- Validates the plaintext SHA-256 checksum\n359\t- Detects corrupted blocks or wrong MEK\n360\t\n361\tExit codes:\n362\t- `0`: Success\n363\t- `1`: Decryption failed (wrong MEK, corrupted data, HMAC mismatch)\n364\t\n365\t### Example Workflow\n366\t\n367\t```bash\n368\t# 1. List objects to find the target\n369\taws s3 ls --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3://bucket/\n370\t\n371\t# 2. Get metadata to see key requirements\n372\taws s3api head-object --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 \\\n373\t  --bucket bucket --key file\n374\t\n375\t# 3. Decrypt with the correct MEK\n376\tarmor-decrypt -mek $ARMOR_MEK -input b2://bucket/file -output recovered\n377\t\n378\t# 4. Verify the recovered file\n379\tsha256sum recovered  # Should match x-amz-meta-armor-plaintext-sha256\n380\t```\n381\t\n382\t## License\n383\t\n384\tMIT\n385\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"6621d013-5048-454e-b1e3-c8b2b8af1737","uuid":"edc9aaad-119c-44b7-9d3c-67bee20d27f4","timestamp":"2026-07-15T15:16:03.213Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/ARMOR/README.md","content":"# ARMOR\n\n**Authenticated Range-readable Managed Object Repository**\n\nARMOR is an S3-compatible proxy server that transparently encrypts data before storing it in [Backblaze B2](https://www.backblaze.com/cloud-storage) and serves downloads through Cloudflare for zero-egress cost. Any S3-compatible client — boto3, AWS CLI, DuckDB, rclone — works without modification.\n\n- **Zero-knowledge encryption** — data is encrypted before it leaves ARMOR; B2 only ever stores ciphertext\n- **Zero egress fees** — downloads route through Cloudflare via the Bandwidth Alliance\n- **Seekable encryption** — AES-256-CTR with 64KB blocks enables byte-range reads without decrypting the whole file\n- **DuckDB-compatible** — query encrypted Parquet files with column pruning and predicate pushdown intact\n- **Multi-key routing** — different master keys for different path prefixes; automatic key selection per object\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Docker\n\n```bash\ndocker run -d \\\n  -p 9000:9000 \\\n  -p 9001:9001 \\\n  -e ARMOR_B2_REGION=us-east-005 \\\n  -e ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your-key-id \\\n  -e ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your-key-secret \\\n  -e ARMOR_BUCKET=your-bucket \\\n  -e ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN=your-cf-domain.example.com \\\n  -e ARMOR_MEK=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \\\n  -e ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY=my-access-key \\\n  -e ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY=my-secret-key \\\n  ronaldraygun/armor:0.1.43\n```\n\n> **Note:** The ARMOR CI pipeline auto-bumps the VERSION file on every build and publishes the container image as `ronaldraygun/armor:<version>` to Docker Hub. Always pin to a specific version tag in production deployments. Use the latest published tag from [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/r/ronaldraygun/armor/tags).\n\n### Client Configuration\n\nPoint any S3-compatible tool at ARMOR's listen address:\n\n```bash\n# AWS CLI\naws --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3 cp file.txt s3://bucket/key\n\n# boto3 (Python)\nimport boto3\ns3 = boto3.client('s3',\n    endpoint_url='http://localhost:9000',\n    aws_access_key_id='my-access-key',\n    aws_secret_access_key='my-secret-key')\ns3.upload_file('local.txt', 'bucket', 'key')\n\n# DuckDB\nINSTALL httpfs;\nLOAD httpfs;\nSET s3_endpoint='localhost:9000';\nSET s3_access_key_id='my-access-key';\nSET s3_secret_access_key='my-secret-key';\nSELECT * FROM read_parquet('s3://bucket/data.parquet');\n```\n\n## Cost Model\n\n| Component | Cost |\n|-----------|------|\n| Storage | ~$6–7/TB/month |\n| Egress (via Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance) | $0 |\n| B2 API calls | $0 |\n| Cloudflare (free plan) | $0 |\n| **Total** | **~$6–7/TB/month** |\n\n## Architecture\n\n### Upload Path (direct to B2 — ingress is free)\n\n```\n┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n│  Client   │────▶│    ARMOR     │────▶│    B2    │\n│           │     │  encrypt +   │     │          │\n│           │     │  upload      │     │          │\n└──────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┘\n```\n\n### Download Path (through Cloudflare — egress is free)\n\n```\n┌──────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n│  Client   │◀───│ Cloudflare │◀───│  Cloudflare │◀───│    B2    │\n│  ARMOR    │    │   Edge     │    │  PNI Link   │    │          │\n│  decrypt  │    │  (cache)   │    │  (free)     │    │          │\n└──────────┘     └────────────┘     └────────────┘     └──────────┘\n```\n\n### DuckDB Query Path (seekable decryption)\n\nDuckDB issues byte-range GET requests for specific row groups and columns. ARMOR decrypts only the requested 64KB blocks, so column pruning and predicate pushdown remain effective:\n\n```\nDuckDB                          ARMOR                       Cloudflare → B2\n  │                                │                              │\n  ├─ read footer (last 8 bytes) ──▶├─ Range GET (encrypted) ────▶│\n  │◀── decrypted footer ──────────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n  │                                │                              │\n  ├─ read col_a, row group 3 ────▶├─ Range GET (3 blocks) ─────▶│\n  │◀── decrypted column chunk ────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n  │                                │                              │\n  └─ result set                    └                              └\n```\n\n## Encryption Design\n\n```\nMaster Key (MEK)\n │  stored locally, never uploaded\n │\n └─▶ wraps ──▶ Data Encryption Key (DEK)\n                │  random per-file, wrapped copy in B2 metadata\n                │\n                └─▶ encrypts ──▶ File Data\n                                   AES-256-CTR, 64KB blocks\n                                   per-block HMAC-SHA256\n                                   seekable random access\n```\n\nKey rotation re-wraps DEKs without re-uploading file data — a metadata-only operation.\n\n## Security Model\n\n| Threat | Mitigation |\n|--------|-----------|\n| B2 breach | Client-side encryption — B2 only stores opaque blobs |\n| Cloudflare inspection | Client-side encryption — Cloudflare only caches opaque blobs |\n| Man-in-the-middle | TLS everywhere + client-side encryption |\n| Key compromise | Envelope encryption — per-file DEKs limit blast radius; rotation re-wraps without re-uploading |\n| Data corruption | SHA-256 integrity hash + per-block HMACs |\n| Unauthorized access | Private bucket + Cloudflare Worker auth + scoped application keys |\n\n## Configuration\n\nARMOR is configured via environment variables:\n\n| Variable | Required | Default | Description |\n|----------|----------|---------|-------------|\n| `ARMOR_LISTEN` | No | `0.0.0.0:9000` | S3 API listen address |\n| `ARMOR_ADMIN_LISTEN` | No | `127.0.0.1:9001` | Admin API (key rotation, canary, audit) |\n| `ARMOR_B2_REGION` | Yes | — | B2 region (e.g., `us-east-005`) |\n| `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | Yes | — | B2 application key ID |\n| `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | Yes | — | B2 application key |\n| `ARMOR_BUCKET` | Yes | — | B2 bucket name |\n| `ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN` | Yes | — | Cloudflare domain CNAME'd to B2 |\n| `ARMOR_MEK` | Yes | — | Master encryption key (hex, 32 bytes) |\n| `ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY` | No | (random) | Client access key |\n| `ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY` | No | (random) | Client secret key |\n| `ARMOR_BLOCK_SIZE` | No | `65536` | Encryption block size (bytes) |\n| `ARMOR_WRITER_ID` | No | (hostname) | Provenance chain writer ID |\n| `ARMOR_READYZ_CACHE_TTL` | No | `30` | Seconds to cache backend connectivity in `/readyz` |\n| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_USER` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth username |\n| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_PASS` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth password |\n| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_TOKEN` | No | — | Dashboard Bearer token |\n\n### Multi-Key Routing\n\nRoute different path prefixes to different master keys:\n\n```bash\nARMOR_MEK=<hex>                           # default key\nARMOR_MEK_SENSITIVE=<hex>                 # named key\nARMOR_MEK_ARCHIVE=<hex>                   # named key\nARMOR_KEY_ROUTES=\"data/pii/*=sensitive,archive/*=archive,*=default\"\n```\n\n### Multi-Credential Configuration\n\n```bash\nARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACCESS_KEY=reader-key\nARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_SECRET_KEY=reader-secret\nARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACL=\"mybucket:readonly/*\"\n\nARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACCESS_KEY=writer-key\nARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_SECRET_KEY=writer-secret\nARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACL=\"mybucket:*,otherbucket:uploads/*\"\n```\n\n## S3 API Coverage\n\n### Transforming Operations (encryption/decryption applied)\n\n| Operation | Support |\n|-----------|---------|\n| PutObject | Full (streaming for large files) |\n| GetObject | Full (range reads) |\n| HeadObject | Full (plaintext size, conditionals) |\n| CopyObject | Full (DEK re-wrapping, cross-bucket) |\n| CreateMultipartUpload | Full |\n| UploadPart | Full |\n| CompleteMultipartUpload | Full |\n| AbortMultipartUpload | Full |\n| ListParts | Full |\n| ListMultipartUploads | Full |\n\n### Passthrough Operations\n\n| Operation | Support |\n|-----------|---------|\n| ListObjectsV2 | Full (size correction, `.armor/` filter) |\n| DeleteObject | Full |\n| DeleteObjects | Full |\n| ListBuckets | Full |\n| CreateBucket / DeleteBucket / HeadBucket | Full |\n| Lifecycle configuration | Full |\n| Object Lock / Retention / Legal Hold | Full |\n\n**Reserved Namespace: `.armor/`**\n\nThe `.armor/` prefix is reserved for ARMOR internal use. Client operations targeting keys with this prefix return `403 AccessDenied`. This protects:\n\n- `.armor/chain/<writer>/*` — Tamper-evident provenance chain entries\n- `.armor/chain-head/<writer>` — Provenance chain head pointers\n- `.armor/manifest/<writer>/*` — Manifest delta files (IV + wrapped DEK entries)\n- `.armor/hmac/<sha256>` — Multipart upload HMAC sidecars\n- `.armor/rotation-state.json` — In-progress key rotation state\n- `.armor/multipart/*.state` — Crash recovery state for multipart uploads\n- `.armor/canary/*` — Health check canary objects\n\nInternal ARMOR components (provenance recorder, manifest persistence, canary, key rotation, multipart state manager) access these keys directly through the backend layer, bypassing the S3 handler guard.\n\n## Web Dashboard\n\nA web dashboard for bucket browsing, encryption status, and metrics is available on the admin port (default `127.0.0.1:9001`):\n\n```bash\nopen http://localhost:9001/dashboard\n```\n\nFeatures:\n- Bucket browsing with prefix-based navigation\n- Encryption status badges per object (key name, ARMOR vs. unencrypted)\n- Metadata cache hit rates\n- Real-time metrics: requests, bytes transferred, uptime, canary status\n\nSee [docs/dashboard.md](docs/dashboard.md) for full documentation.\n\n## Admin API\n\nKey management and monitoring endpoints on the admin listener (`127.0.0.1:9001`):\n\n| Endpoint | Method | Description |\n|----------|--------|-------------|\n| `/healthz` | GET | Liveness check |\n| `/readyz` | GET | Readiness check (verifies B2 connectivity) |\n| `/metrics` | GET | Prometheus metrics |\n| `/admin/key/verify` | GET | Verify MEK can decrypt the canary object |\n| `/admin/key/rotate` | POST | Rotate MEK — re-wraps all DEKs, no file re-upload |\n| `/admin/key/export` | GET | Export current MEK (`?confirm=yes`) |\n| `/admin/audit` | GET | Walk provenance chains, verify integrity |\n| `/admin/presign` | POST | Generate pre-signed share URL |\n| `/armor/canary` | GET | Canary integrity status |\n| `/dashboard` | GET | Web dashboard |\n\n## Repository Structure\n\n```\nARMOR/\n├── cmd/armor/main.go          # Entrypoint\n├── internal/\n│   ├── server/                # S3 API handlers, auth\n│   ├── crypto/                # Encryption, decryption, envelope key management\n│   ├── backend/               # B2 S3 client, Cloudflare download routing\n│   ├── canary/                # Self-healing integrity monitor\n│   ├── config/                # Configuration loading (env vars)\n│   ├── keymanager/            # Multi-key routing\n│   ├── dashboard/             # Web dashboard UI and metrics\n│   ├── presign/               # Pre-signed URL generation\n│   ├── provenance/            # Cryptographic audit chain\n│   ├── logging/               # Structured JSON logging\n│   └── metrics/               # Prometheus metrics\n├── deploy/kubernetes/         # Kubernetes manifests\n├── tests/integration/         # Integration tests (requires real B2 + Cloudflare)\n└── docs/\n    ├── dashboard.md\n    ├── cloudflare-setup.md    # DNS configuration for zero-egress downloads\n    └── research/\n```\n\n## Documentation\n\n- [Disaster Recovery](docs/disaster-recovery.md) — MEK backup/escrow, restore drills, rotation failure recovery\n- [Web Dashboard](docs/dashboard.md) — Bucket browsing, encryption status, cache statistics\n- [Cloudflare Setup](docs/cloudflare-setup.md) — DNS configuration for zero-egress B2 downloads\n- [Integration Tests](tests/integration/README.md) — Testing against real B2 + Cloudflare\n\n## Disaster Recovery / Offline Decryption\n\nARMOR includes a standalone CLI tool `armor-decrypt` for recovering encrypted objects without a running ARMOR server. This enables disaster recovery scenarios where you have:\n\n- The Master Encryption Key (MEK)\n- Access to B2 (or a local copy of an encrypted object)\n\n### Building the Decrypt Tool\n\n```bash\ngo build -o armor-decrypt ./cmd/armor-decrypt\n```\n\n### Usage\n\n#### Decrypt from B2\n\n```bash\n# Decrypt directly from B2 (requires B2 credentials)\narmor-decrypt \\\n  -mek 0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef \\\n  -input b2://my-bucket/path/to/file.encrypted \\\n  -output recovered-file.txt\n\n# Using MEK from environment\nexport ARMOR_MEK=0123456789abcdef...\narmor-decrypt -input b2://my-bucket/file -output recovered.txt\n\n# With verbose output\narmor-decrypt -mek HEX -input b2://bucket/file -v - output recovered.txt\n```\n\n#### Decrypt from Local File\n\nFor local files, you need the wrapped DEK (from `x-amz-meta-armor-wrapped-dek` metadata):\n\n```bash\narmor-decrypt \\\n  -mek 0123456789abcdef... \\\n  -input /path/to/encrypted.bin \\\n  -wrapped-dek WWF...base64... \\\n  -output plaintext.bin\n```\n\n### Key Requirements\n\n- **MEK (Master Encryption Key)**: 32-byte hex string\n- **For B2**: `ARMOR_B2_REGION`, `ARMOR_B2_ENDPOINT`, `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`\n- **For local files**: Wrapped DEK (base64, from object metadata)\n\n### Multi-Key Support\n\nIf your ARMOR deployment uses named keys (via `ARMOR_KEY_ROUTES`), specify the key ID:\n\n```bash\narmor-decrypt \\\n  -mek <hex-for-specific-key> \\\n  -input b2://bucket/file \\\n  -key-id sensitive \\\n  -output recovered.txt\n```\n\nThe key ID comes from the `x-amz-meta-armor-key-id` metadata header.\n\n### Verification\n\nThe decrypt tool automatically:\n\n- Verifies per-block HMAC-SHA256 integrity\n- Validates the plaintext SHA-256 checksum\n- Detects corrupted blocks or wrong MEK\n\nExit codes:\n- `0`: Success\n- `1`: Decryption failed (wrong MEK, corrupted data, HMAC mismatch)\n\n### Example Workflow\n\n```bash\n# 1. List objects to find the target\naws s3 ls --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3://bucket/\n\n# 2. Get metadata to see key requirements\naws s3api head-object --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 \\\n  --bucket bucket --key file\n\n# 3. Decrypt with the correct MEK\narmor-decrypt -mek $ARMOR_MEK -input b2://bucket/file -output recovered\n\n# 4. Verify the recovered file\nsha256sum recovered  # Should match x-amz-meta-armor-plaintext-sha256\n```\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n","numLines":385,"startLine":1,"totalLines":385}}}

It mirrors manifest.Entry but is defined here to avoid an\n39\t// import cycle between the handlers and manifest packages.\n40\ttype ManifestEntry struct {\n41\t\tPlaintextSize int64\n42\t\tContentType   string\n43\t\tETag          string\n44\t\tLastModified  time.Time\n45\t\tIV            []byte\n46\t\tWrappedDEK    []byte\n47\t\tBlockSize     int\n48\t}\n49\t\n50\t// ManifestRecorder records successful S3 write operations in the manifest\n51\t// index for fast in-memory lookup and enqueues them for async B2 persistence\n52\t// as delta files. Implementations must be safe for concurrent use.\n53\ttype ManifestRecorder interface {\n54\t\t// RecordPut records a successful PutObject or CompleteMultipartUpload.\n55\t\tRecordPut(bucket, key string, size int64, sha256Hex string, iv, wrappedDEK []byte, blockSize int, contentType, etag string)\n56\t\t// RecordDelete records a successful DeleteObject.\n57\t\tRecordDelete(bucket, key string)\n58\t\t// Lookup returns manifest metadata for bucket/key, or (nil, false) if not\n59\t\t// tracked. routes S3 operations based on the request.\n103\tfunc (h *Handlers) HandleRoot(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {\n104\t\tpath := r.URL.Path\n105\t\n106\t\t// Parse bucket and key from path\n107\t\t// Path format: /bucket/key or /bucket\n108\t\tpath = strings.TrimPrefix(path, \"/\")\n109\t\tparts := strings.SplitN(path, \"/\", 2)\n110\t\n111\t\tbucket := \"\"\n112\t\tkey := \"\"\n113\t\tif len(parts) > 0 && parts[0] != \"\" {\n114\t\t\tbucket = parts[0]\n115\t\t}\n116\t\tif len(parts) > 1 {\n117\t\t\tkey = parts[1]\n118\t\t\t// URL decode the key (DuckDB httpfs encodes special chars like = as %3D)\n119\t\t\tif decoded, err := url.PathUnescape(key); err == nil {\n120\t\t\t\tkey = decoded\n121\t\t\t}\n122\t\t}\n123\t\n124\t\t// Protect the .armor/ reserved namespace\n125\t\t// Client operations targeting keys with this prefix return 403 AccessDenied\n126\t\tif strings.HasPrefix(key, \".armor/\") {\n127\t\t\th.writeError(w, \"AccessDenied\", \"Access to .armor/ reserved namespace is denied\", 403)\n128\t\t\treturn\n129\t\t}\n130\t\n131\t\t// Route based on method and path\n132\t\tswitch r.Method {\n133\t\tcase http.MethodGet:\n134\t\t\t// Handle {\n141\t\t\t\th.ListObjectVersions(w, r, bucket)\n142\t\t\t\treturn\n143\t\t\t}\n144\t\t\t// Handle GetBucketLifecycleConfiguration (GET ?lifecycle on bucket)\n145\t\t\tif bucket)\n152\t\t\t\treturn\n153\t\t\t}\n154\t\t\t// Handle GetObjectRetention (GET ?retention on object)\n155\t\t\tif r.URL.Query().Has(\"retention\") && key != \"\" {\n156\t\t\t\th.GetObjectRetention(w, r, bucket, key)\n157\t\t\t\treturn\n158\t\t\t}\n159\t\t\t// Handle GetObjectLegalHold (GET ?legal-hold on object)\n160\t\t\tif r.URL.Query().Has(\"legal-hold\") && key != \"\" {\n161\t\t\t\th.GetObjectLegalHold(w, r, bucket, key)\n162\t\t\t\treturn\n163\t\t\t}\n164\t\t\t// Handle ListParts (GET ?uploadId on object)\n165\t\t\tif uploadID := r.URL.Query().Get(\"uploadId\"); uploadID != \"\" && key != \"\" {\n166\t\t\t\th.ListParts(w, r, bucket, key, uploadID)\n167\t\t\t\treturn\n168\t\t\t}\n169\t\t\t// Regular Get operations\n170\t\t\tif key != \"\" {\n171\t\t\t\th.GetObject(w, r, bucket, key)\n172\t\t\t} else if bucket != \"\" {\n173\t\t\t\tq := r.URL.Query()\n174\t\t\t\tswitch {\n175\t\t\t\tcase q.Has(\"location\"):\n176\t\t\t\t\th.GetBucketLocation(w, r, bucket)\n177\t\t\t\tcase q.Has(\"versioning\"):\n178\t\t\t\t\th.GetBucketVersioning(w, r, bucket)\n179\t\t\t\tdefault:\n180\t\t\t\t\t// All other bucket-level GETs are list operations.\n181\t\t\t\t\t// HeadBucket is only for HTTP HEAD method (handled in MethodHead case).\n182\t\t\t\t\th.ListObjectsV2(w, r, bucket)\n183\t\t\t\t}\n184\t\t\t} else {\n185\t\t\t\th.ListBuckets(w, r)\n186\t\t\t}\n187\t\tcase http.MethodPut:\n188\t\t\t// Handle PutBucketLifecycleConfiguration (PUT ?lifecycle on
